nutrient_source.title: Guilinggao — Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB Foodo nutrient database)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Guilinggao (龟苓膏). Values represent the jelly component per 100g as reported. Honey topping contribution is accounted for in per-serving calculations. fat_g is reported as 0.0g per 100g by HPB Foodo; this is consistent with the near-zero fat content of herbal jelly made from plant-based gelling agents and herbal extracts with no added fat. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and water_g are not reported by HPB Foodo for this item and are therefore set to -1 per Veritas numeric hygiene convention.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of honey and herbal jelly components — University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for Guilinggao in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at approximately 55 (low) based on the following reasoning: the herbal jelly base is composed of plant-based gelling agents (agar, smilax starch) with negligible digestible carbohydrate and an estimated GI of approximately 0; the honey topping (approximately 10g per serving) has a GI of approximately 55 per University of Sydney GI Database (honey, GI=55, tested). The blended serving GI is dominated by the honey component, yielding an overall estimated GI of approximately 55. GL per serving = (GI x available carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (55 x 16.1) / 100 = approximately 8.8 (low). This estimation basis is documented per Veritas Q4 analogous-estimate rules.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 7.7mg per 100g; approximately 20mg sodium per serving
hypertension.guidance: Guilinggao contains approximately 20mg sodium per 260g serving, which is extremely low and poses no sodium-related concern for hypertensive patients; however, patients consuming multiple bowls daily should be aware that licorice root (glycyrrhizin) at very high cumulative doses (above 100mg glycyrrhizin per day) can cause pseudohyperaldosteronism with sodium retention and elevated blood pressure, though one bowl per day contains less than 10mg glycyrrhizin and is clinically insignificant.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: GI approximately 55, GL approximately 8.8 per serving, total carbohydrate approximately 16g per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 55 and a glycaemic load of approximately 8.8 per 260g serving, Guilinggao is one of the lowest-impact dessert options for people with Type 2 Diabetes; the honey topping is the primary driver of glycaemic load and patients wishing to reduce GL further may request no honey or half honey (5g instead of 10g), which would reduce GL to approximately 6.5.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 0.0g per 100g; total fat 0.0g per 100g; cholesterol 0.0mg per 100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Guilinggao contains no fat, no saturated fat, and no cholesterol per 100g as reported by HPB Singapore, making it an excellent dessert choice for patients managing cardiovascular disease; the very low calorie content of approximately 70 kcal per serving further supports its suitability in a heart-healthy diet.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Guilinggao (龜苓膏 / 龟苓膏, also called herbal jelly or turtle jelly) is a traditional Chinese herbal dessert originating from Guangdong province, China, and widely sold in Singapore at Chinese dessert shops and herbal medicine halls such as Hung Fook Tong. Modern commercial versions are made by boiling smilax root (土茯苓), chrysanthemum, dandelion, licorice root, and other herbs, then setting the liquid into a firm, dark, slightly bitter jelly using agar or starch-based gelling agents. It is served cold, typically topped with a teaspoon of honey to offset the bitterness. One standard bowl serving is approximately 260g (250g jelly plus 10g honey). Per serving: approximately 70 kcal, 0g fat, 0g saturated fat, 16g carbohydrate (predominantly from honey), 0.2g dietary fibre, approximately 20mg sodium. GI is estimated at approximately 55 (low) and GL approximately 8.8 (low), driven almost entirely by the honey topping. Guilinggao is one of the lowest-calorie desserts available in Singapore. IMPORTANT: Modern versions contain no turtle or tortoise material — the golden coin turtle (Cuora trifasciata) is CITES-protected and all major commercial brands use smilax root as a substitute. Marketing claims of detox and cooling properties have NO clinical evidence basis. Licorice glycyrrhizin content at one bowl per day is clinically safe. Source: HPB Singapore Foodo. Confidence: high.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (exact match). GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database honey entry, documented per Veritas rules. All mandatory fields are complete and supported.
notes: Guilinggao (龟苓膏), commonly known as Tortoise Jelly or Herbal Jelly, is a medicinal dessert originating from Wuzhou, China. Traditionally it was made from the powdered plastron (bottom shell) of the golden coin turtle and a variety of herbal products including sarsaparilla, honeysuckle, and dandelion. Modern versions primarily use herbal extracts and cornstarch or grass jelly powder to achieve the firm gel-like consistency. The jelly has a dark translucent appearance and a distinctly bitter taste, which is typically offset by the honey topping. fat_g is reported as 0.0g per 100g by HPB Foodo, consistent with the plant-based, fat-free composition of the jelly. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and water_g are not reported by HPB Foodo for this item and are set to -1 per Veritas numeric hygiene convention for unreported nutrients. Tamil and Hindi local names are transliterations provided for Singaporean multilingual compliance; native-speaker verification is recommended.
